records.ety: [L. respectare, v. intens. from respicere, respectum, to look back, respect; pref. re- re- + specere, spicere, to look, to view: cf. F. respecter. See Spy, and cf. Respite.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Respected; p. pr. & vb. n. Respecting.]

defs: To take notice of; to regard with special attention; to regard as worthy of special consideration; hence, to care for; to heed.
quotes: Thou respectest not spilling Edward's blood.
In orchards and gardens, we do not so much respect beauty as variety of ground for fruits, trees, and herbs.

defs: To consider worthy of esteem; to regard with honor.
To look toward; to front upon or toward.
quotes: Palladius adviseth the front of his house should so respect the truth.

defs: To regard; to consider; to deem.
quotes: To whom my father gave this name of Gaspar, And as his own respected him to death.

defs: To have regard to; to have reference to; to relate to; as, the treaty particularly respects our commerce.

records.ety: [L. respectus: cf. F. respect. See Respect, v., and cf. Respite.]

defs: The act of noticing with attention; the giving particular consideration to; hence, care; caution.
quotes: But he it well did ward with wise respect.
Seen without awe, and served without respect.
The same men treat the Lord's Day with as little respect.

defs: An expression of respect of deference; regards; as, to send one's respects to another.

quotes: Many of the best respect in Rome.
They believed but one Supreme Deity, which, with respect to the various benefits men received from him, had several titles.

defs: Particular; point regarded; point of view; as, in this respect; in any respect; in all respects.
quotes: Everything which is imperfect, as the world must be acknowledged in many respects.
In one respect I'll be thy assistant.
To the publik good Private respects must yield.
